sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Telecommunications Act 1997 commencement and objects
sections.summary: This section outlines the commencement dates for the Telecommunications Act 1997 and its primary objectives. The Act commences on the day of Royal Assent for most provisions, with specific sections starting on June 5, 1997, or July 1, 1997. Its main purpose is to promote end-user interests, industry efficiency, and affordable telecommunications services in Australia, aligned with the Competition and Consumer Act 2010.

requirements.text: This Act may be cited as the Telecommunications Act 1997
Sections 52 to 55 commence on 5 June 1997
The remaining provisions commence on 1 July 1997

sections.source_quote: This Act may be cited as the Telecommunications Act 1997. Sections 52 to 55 (inclusive) commence on 5 June 1997. The remaining provisions of this Act commence on 1 July 1997.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Telecommunications Act 1997 regulatory policy and service providers
sections.summary: This section details the regulatory policy for telecommunications, emphasizing industry self-regulation and minimal administrative burdens. It specifies that telecommunications must be regulated in a way that promotes industry self-regulation without imposing undue financial or administrative costs, while ensuring regulatory effectiveness for achieving the Act's stated objectives.

requirements.text: Telecommunications must be regulated to promote the greatest practicable use of industry self-regulation
Regulation must not impose undue financial or administrative burdens on industry participants

sections.source_quote: The Parliament intends that telecommunications be regulated in a manner that: (a) promotes the greatest practicable use of industry self-regulation; and (b) does not impose undue financial and administrative burdens on participants in the Australian telecommunications industry; but does not compromise the effectiveness of regulation in achieving the objects mentioned in section 3.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Telecommunications Act 1997 service provider definitions and ACMA responsibilities
sections.summary: This section defines key telecommunications entities, including carriers, carriage service providers, content service providers, and outlines ACMA's responsibilities. It specifies that carriers must hold licenses for network units, service providers must comply with rules, and ACMA must monitor carrier performance and establish registries like the SMS Sender ID Register.

requirements.text: A carrier is the holder of a carrier licence granted under this Act
The owner of a network unit must hold a carrier licence unless responsibility is transferred
Carriers and carriage service providers must protect the confidentiality of communications
ACMA must monitor and report significant matters relating to carrier performance annually
ACMA must ensure the SMS Sender ID Register is established and maintained

sections.source_quote: The ACMA is to monitor, and report each year to the Minister on, significant matters relating to the performance of carriers and carriage service providers. The ACMA must ensure that the SMS Sender ID Register is established and maintained.

sections.heading: Technical regulation
sections.title: Telecommunications Act 1997 definitions for telecommunications entities
sections.summary: This section provides detailed definitions for telecommunications entities, including carriers, carriage service providers, content service providers, and regulatory bodies like ACMA and ACCC. It clarifies terms such as 'carriage service', 'carriage service provider', 'carrier', and 'carriage licence', which are critical for understanding the Act's scope and obligations.

requirements.text: ACMA means the Australian Communications and Media Authority
ACMA's telecommunications functions means the functions that are telecommunications functions for the ACMA under the Australian Communications and Media Authority Act 2005
ACMA's telecommunications powers means the powers conferred on the ACMA by specific Acts
Carriage service means a service for carrying communications by means of guided and/or unguided electromagnetic energy
Carriage service provider has the meaning given by section 87

sections.source_quote: In this Act, unless the contrary intention appears: ACCC means the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission. ACMA means the Australian Communications and Media Authority. Carriage service means a service for carrying communications by means of guided and/or unguided electromagnetic energy.

sections.heading: Technical regulation (cont.)
sections.title: Technical definitions for telecommunications services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in telecommunications regulation, including network units, facilities, carriage services, and industry-specific terms. It specifies that certain entities like police forces and emergency services qualify as exempt network-users if they use network units for communication purposes. The definitions cover technical infrastructure, service providers, and regulatory frameworks relevant to telecommunications operations in Australia.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish the technical scope for telecommunications services, which is critical for determining what constitutes cross-border data transfers under international regulations. They help clarify when data flows between entities in Australia and other jurisdictions.

requirements.text: exempt network-user means a person who is entitled to use a network unit to supply a carriage service and whose entitlement derives from rights granted to a carrier
exempt network-user includes police forces, fire services, ambulance services, and emergency services using network units for communication between members or with other forces
facility means any part of telecommunications infrastructure or equipment used in a network
internet carriage service means a service enabling end-users to access the internet
line means a physical medium used for carrying communications

sections.source_quote: Section 7: exempt network-user means: (a) a person who is entitled to use a network unit to supply a carriage service; and whose entitlement derives, directly or indirectly, from rights granted to a carrier; or (b) if a police force or service uses a network unit for communication between members or with other forces...

sections.heading: Technical regulation (cont.)
sections.title: Technical definitions for telecommunications services
sections.summary: This section provides additional technical definitions for telecommunications services, including terms like telecommunications network, carriage services, and specific service types. It clarifies that certain communications activities, such as telemarketing calls, are defined based on their purpose and content. The definitions help regulate how telecommunications services operate across different jurisdictions.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are essential for understanding what constitutes a telecommunications service that may involve cross-border data transfers, particularly when services operate between Australia and other countries.

requirements.text: telecommunications network means a system carrying communications by guided and/or unguided electromagnetic energy
telemarketing call means a call made to an Australian number for opinion polling or standard questionnaire-based research
standard questionnaire-based research means research involving standard questions without opinion polling

sections.source_quote: Section 7: telecommunications network means a system, or series of systems, that carries, or is capable of carrying, communications by means of guided and/or unguided electromagnetic energy.

sections.heading: Technical regulation (cont.)
sections.title: Technical regulation definitions for telecommunications networks
sections.summary: This section defines technical terms related to telecommunications networks, specifically customer cabling and customer equipment. It establishes that customer cabling refers to lines used on the customer side of a telecommunications network boundary, while customer equipment includes any apparatus or systems used on the customer side. The boundary of a telecommunications network is defined by agreements between carriers, service providers, or customers, with specific rules for physical locations like buildings and satellite facilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines technical boundaries for telecommunications networks, which is critical for determining where data processing occurs within Australia's network infrastructure. This helps in assessing data residency and cross-border transfer implications for telecommunications data.

requirements.text: Customer cabling means a line used, installed ready for use or intended for use on the customer side of the boundary of a telecommunications network

Customer equipment means any equipment, apparatus, tower, mast, antenna or other structure or thing used, installed ready for use or intended for use on the customer side of the boundary of a telecommunications network
The boundary of a telecommunications network is ascertained in accordance with regulations

sections.source_quote: Section 20: For the purposes of this Act, customer cabling means a line that, under the regulations, is treated as customer cabling. Section 21: For the purposes of this Act, customer equipment means: (a) any equipment, apparatus, tower, mast, antenna or other structure or thing; or (b) any system (whether software-based or otherwise); that: (c) is used, installed ready for use or intended for use in connection with a carriage service; and (d) under the regulations, is treated as customer equipment; but does not include a line.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Compilation of telecommunications network boundaries
sections.summary: This section provides detailed rules for determining the boundary of a telecommunications network, particularly for customer-side equipment and cabling. It specifies that the boundary can be defined by agreements between carriers, service providers, or customers, with specific rules for physical locations like buildings and satellite facilities. The section also includes provisions for how boundaries are treated in cases where no regulations are in force.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines precise network boundaries to determine where data processing occurs, which is essential for assessing data residency and cross-border transfer implications in telecommunications services.

requirements.text: The boundary of a telecommunications network is ascertained in accordance with regulations

In cases where a line enters a building, the boundary is the point agreed between the customer and carrier or service provider, or if no agreement, the side of the main distribution frame nearest to the end-user
In cases where a satellite-based facility is used, the boundary is the outer surface of the satellite-based facility

sections.source_quote: Section 22: For the purposes of sections 20, 21, 30, 349, 372B, 372C and 372V, the boundary of a telecommunications network is to be ascertained in accordance with the regulations. Section 22: In a case where a telecommunications network is used to supply a carriage service to an end-user in a building by means of a line that enters the building—the point agreed between the customer and the carrier or carriage service provider who operates the telecommunications network, or, failing agreement: (i) if there is a main distribution frame in the building and the line is connected to the frame—the side of the frame nearest to the end-user; or (ii) if subparagraph (i) does not apply but the line is connected to a network termination device located in, on or within close proximity to, the building—the side of the device nearest to the end-user; or (iii) if neither subparagraph (i) nor (ii) applies but the line is connected to one or more sockets in the building—the side nearest to the end-user of the first socket after the building entry point; or (b) in a case where a telecommunications network is used to supply a carriage service to an end-user by means of a satellite-based facility that transmits to, or receives transmissions from, the point where the end user is located—the outer surface of the satellite-based facility

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions for core functions and related entities
sections.summary: This section defines key terms such as 'core function' for authorities and institutions, 'director', 'officer', and 'tertiary education institution'. It specifies that a core function is a primary function rather than a secondary or incidental one, and provides definitions for various roles within government entities and organizations.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines organizational structures and roles relevant to data processing, which helps in understanding who is responsible for data handling and how data flows within government entities.

requirements.text: Core function, in relation to an authority or institution, means a function of the authority or institution other than a secondary or incidental function
Director includes a constituent member of a body corporate incorporated for a public purpose by a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory

Executive officer, in relation to a body corporate, means a person, by whatever name called and whether or not a director of the body, who is concerned in, or takes part in, the management of the body

sections.source_quote: Section 23: In this section: core function, in relation to an authority or institution, means a function of the authority or institution other than a secondary or incidental function. director includes a constituent member of a body corporate incorporated for a public purpose by a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exemptions for intelligence and transport operations
sections.summary: This section outlines exemptions from certain telecommunications regulations for specific entities, including intelligence agencies and transport authorities. It specifies that networks used by the Australian Secret Intelligence Service, Australian Security Intelligence Organisation, and Australian Signals Directorate are exempt from certain conditions. It also provides exemptions for transport authorities that use networks for aviation services or train/bus services.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides exemptions for specific telecommunications networks used by intelligence agencies and transport authorities, which may impact data transfer assessments for these entities.

requirements.text: Section 42 does not apply to a network unit used wholly or principally by the Australian Secret Intelligence Service
Section 42 does not apply to a network unit used wholly or principally by the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation
Section 42 does not apply to a network unit used wholly or principally by the Australian Signals Directorate
Section 42 does not apply to a network unit if the sole use is by Airservices Australia to carry communications necessary for aviation services
Section 42 does not apply to a network unit if the sole use is by a State or Territory transport authority to carry communications for train, bus, or tram services
Section 42 does not apply to a network unit if the sole use is by a rail corporation to carry communications for train services

sections.source_quote: Section 46: Section 42 does not apply to a network unit that is used wholly or principally: (a) by the Australian Secret Intelligence Service; or (b) by the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation; or (c) by the Australian Signals Directorate. Section 47: Section 42 does not apply to a network unit if the sole use of the unit is use by Airservices Australia to carry communications necessary or desirable for the workings of aviation services.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Conditions for carrier licences and notifications
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions for carrier licences, including the requirement to notify applicants of conditions when an instrument is made. It specifies that the validity of an instrument is not affected by failure to notify conditions, and that instruments take effect when the licence is granted.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the conditions under which carrier licences are granted and the notification requirements, which are critical for assessing the legal framework governing telecommunications data transfers.

requirements.text: The Minister must give the applicant for the licence a copy of the instrument as soon as practicable after making it
A contravention of subsection (7) or (8) does not affect the validity of an instrument
An instrument under subsection (3) relating to a licence takes effect when the licence is granted

sections.source_quote: Section 82: (8) As soon as practicable after the Minister makes an instrument under subsection (3) that relates to a licence, the Minister must give the applicant for the licence a copy of the instrument.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Cancellation of carrier licences and submissions
sections.summary: This section details the process for cancelling carrier licences, including the requirement to give written notice of cancellation to the carrier, the time limit for submissions, and the conditions under which a licence can be cancelled.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides the process for cancelling carrier licences, which is relevant for data transfer assessments when a telecommunications provider ceases operations.

requirements.text: The ACMA must not cancel a carrier licence unless it has first given the carrier written notice setting out a proposal to cancel and inviting a submission
A time limit specified in the notice must run for at least 7 days
If a carrier licence is cancelled, the ACMA must give written notice of the cancellation to the person

sections.source_quote: Section 94: (6) The ACMA must not cancel a carrier licence under subsection (1), (2), (3) or (4) unless the ACMA has first: (a) given the carrier a written notice: (i) setting out a proposal to cancel the licence; and (ii) inviting the carrier to make a submission to the ACMA on the proposal; and (b) considered any submission that was received within the time limit.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Recovery of charges and penalties by ACMA
sections.summary: The ACMA, acting on behalf of the Commonwealth, may recover annual charges, application charges, and late payment penalties as debts due to the Commonwealth. These recovered amounts must be paid to the Commonwealth. A determination under subsections (3) or (4) constitutes a legislative instrument.

requirements.text: ACMA may recover annual charge, application charge and late payment penalty as debts due to Commonwealth
Amounts received must be paid to Commonwealth
Determination under subsection (3) or (4) is a legislative instrument

sections.source_quote: Annual charge, application charge and late payment penalty may be recovered by the ACMA, on behalf of the Commonwealth, as debts due to the Commonwealth. Amounts received by way of annual charge, application charge or late payment penalty must be paid to the Commonwealth.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Cancellation of exemptions from carrier licence charges
sections.summary: This section cancels exemptions from liability to pay charges under the Telecommunications (Carrier Licence Charges) Act 1997. The cancellation does not apply if the exempting provision was enacted after this section came into force and specifically refers to the charges in question.

requirements.text: This section cancels effect of provisions exempting a person from liability to pay charge under Telecommunications (Carrier Licence Charges) Act 1997
Cancellation does not apply if exempting provision enacted after commencement of this section and refers specifically to charge under Telecommunications (Carrier Licence Charges) Act 1997

sections.source_quote: This section cancels the effect of a provision of another Act that would have the effect of exempting a person from liability to pay charge imposed by the Telecommunications (Carrier Licence Charges) Act 1997.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Nominated carrier declarations applications
sections.summary: Carriers may apply to the ACMA for nominated carrier declarations for specified network units. Applications must include any applicable charge, consent from network unit owners, and an election accepting responsibility for the units. Applications must follow ACMA-approved forms.

requirements.text: Carrier may apply to ACMA for nominated carrier declaration in relation to one or more specified network units
Application must be accompanied by charge fixed by determination under section 60 of Australian Communications and Media Authority Act 2005
Application must be accompanied by consent of owner or each owner of network units
Application must be accompanied by election of applicant accepting responsibility for units
Application, consent and election must be in accordance with ACMA-approved form

sections.source_quote: A carrier may apply to the ACMA for a nominated carrier declaration in relation to one or more specified network units.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Notification of refusal of nominated carrier applications
sections.summary: If the ACMA refuses a nominated carrier declaration application, it must provide written notice to the applicant and the owners of the network units. This ensures transparency and accountability in the refusal process.

requirements.text: ACMA must give written notice of refusal to applicant
ACMA must give written notice of refusal to owner or each owner of network units

sections.source_quote: If the ACMA refuses to make a nominated carrier declaration, the ACMA must give written notice of the refusal to: (a) the applicant; and (b) the owner, or each of the owners, of the network units.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Register of nominated carrier declarations and carrier licences
sections.summary: The ACMA maintains a Register containing all current nominated carrier declarations, carrier licences, and their conditions. The Register may be accessed electronically, and persons may inspect or copy it upon payment of any applicable charge.

requirements.text: ACMA includes all nominated carrier declarations currently in force in Register
ACMA includes all carrier licences currently in force in Register
ACMA includes all conditions of such licences in Register
Register may be maintained by electronic means
Person may inspect Register on payment of charge fixed by determination under section 60
Person may make copy of or take extracts from Register on payment of charge
For electronic Register, person is taken to have made copy if ACMA provides printout
ACMA may provide information electronically if person requests electronic form

sections.source_quote: The ACMA is to maintain a Register in which the ACMA includes: (a) all nominated carrier declarations currently in force; and (b) all carrier licences currently in force; and (c) all conditions of such licences.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Defence organisation definitions
sections.summary: This section defines 'defence organisation' to include the Defence Department, Australian Defence Force, foreign organisations with similar functions authorized to operate in Australia, and parts of such organisations. The definition is used to determine exemptions for intelligence operations and transport authorities.

requirements.text: Defence organisation means: (a) the Defence Department; or (b) the Australian Defence Force; or (c) an organisation of a foreign country so far as it has functions corresponding to Defence Department or Australian Defence Force and is authorized by Commonwealth to operate or train in Australia
Defence organisation includes a part of such an organisation or body

sections.source_quote: defence organisation means: (a) the Defence Department; or (b) the Australian Defence Force; or (c) an organisation of a foreign country, so far as the organisation: (i) has functions corresponding to functions of, or of a part of, the Defence Department or the Australian Defence Force; and (ii) is authorised by the Commonwealth to operate or train in Australia or an external Territory; or (d) a part of such an organisation or body.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions for broadcasting transmitters and cable systems
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in telecommunications, including 'broadcasting transmitter' as a radiocommunications transmitter delivering broadcasting services, and 'head end of a cable transmission system' as a facility connected to a line link that processes signals for end-users. It also specifies exemptions for electricity supply bodies and ministerial determinations regarding eligible definitions.

requirements.text: broadcasting transmitter means a radiocommunications transmitter used, or for use, to deliver a broadcasting service

head end of a cable transmission system means a facility that: (a) is connected to a line link; (b) is used, or for use, in connection with the delivery of the service; (c) processes signals for delivery by the line link to end-users having equipment appropriate for receiving the service

electricity supply body means an authority, or a body corporate, that carries on a business, or performs a function, of: (a) generating, transmitting, distributing or supplying electricity; or (b) managing the generation, transmission, distribution or supply of electricity

sections.source_quote: broadcasting transmitter means a radiocommunications transmitter used, or for use, to deliver a broadcasting service. head end of a cable transmission system means a facility that: (a) is connected to a line link; and (b) is used, or for use, in connection with the delivery of the service; (c) processes signals for delivery by the line link to end-users having equipment appropriate for receiving the service.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: ACCC's authority to issue formal warnings for rule violations
sections.summary: This section grants the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) the authority to issue formal warnings when individuals or entities violate specific service provider rules outlined in various sections of the Telecommunications Act. The warnings apply to contraventions of rules related to sections 152BED(2), 152CJD(2), 38(2), and Part 1 of Schedule 2, particularly concerning compliance with telecommunications regulations.

requirements.text: The ACCC may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes the service provider rule set out in subsection 152BED(2) of the Telecommunications Act
The ACCC may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes the service provider rule set out in subsection 152CJD(2) of the Telecommunications Act
The ACCC may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes the service provider rule set out in subsection 38(2) of the National Telecommunications Act

The ACCC may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes the service provider rule set out in Part 1 of Schedule 2 in so far as that rule relates to section 142C, 143, 143B, 151ZA, 151ZB, 151ZD, 151ZF, 151ZG, 151ZH or 151ZI
The ACCC may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes the service provider rule set out in Part 1 of Schedule 2 in so far as that rule relates to section 369

sections.source_quote: The ACCC may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes the service provider rule set out in subsection 152BED(2) of the Telecommunications Act.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: ACMA must consider benefits and business interests when compiling industry codes
sections.summary: The ACMA must consider the number of persons likely to benefit from industry codes, whether those persons are householders or small business operators, and the legitimate business interests of participants in the fax marketing industry when compiling industry codes. This section does not limit the matters that may be considered for compilation purposes.

requirements.text: ACMA must consider the number of persons likely to benefit from industry codes
ACMA must consider whether those persons are householders or small business operators
ACMA must consider the legitimate business interests of participants in the fax marketing industry

sections.source_quote: administrative burdens on participants in sections of the fax marketing industry, the ACMA must have regard to: (a) the number of persons who would be likely to benefit from (b) the extent to which those persons are householders or small business operators; and (c) the legitimate business interests of participants in sections of the fax marketing industry.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Examples of matters for industry codes and standards in telecommunications
sections.summary: This section lists examples of matters that industry codes and standards may address, including customer information about goods/services, performance indicators, complaint handling, privacy protections, telecommunications monitoring, directory services, customer churning, security deposits, debt collection, and billing accuracy. The applicability of these examples depends on the specific industry involved.

requirements.text: Industry codes may cover telling customers about goods or services on offer
Industry codes may cover pricing information for goods or services
Industry codes may cover terms and conditions for goods or services
Industry codes may cover customer information about performance indicators
Industry codes may cover regular reporting to customers about performance against indicators
Industry codes may cover internal handling of customer complaints
Industry codes may cover reporting about customer complaints
Industry codes may cover privacy protections including personal information
Industry codes may cover intrusive use of telecommunications by carriers
Industry codes may cover monitoring or recording of communications
Industry codes may cover calling number display
Industry codes may cover directory products and services
Industry codes may cover customer churning practices
Industry codes may cover security deposits given by customers
Industry codes may cover debt collection practices
Industry codes may cover customer credit practices
Industry codes may cover disconnection of customers
Industry codes may cover ensuring customers have informed basis for agreements
Industry codes may cover quality of standard telephone services
Industry codes may cover accuracy of billing for carriage services
Industry codes may cover timeliness and comprehensibility of bills
Industry codes may cover procedures for generating standard billing reports
Industry codes may cover design features of optical fibre lines
Industry codes may cover facilities used in optical fibre lines
Industry codes may cover performance requirements for optical fibre lines
Industry codes may cover facilities used in optical fibre lines
Industry codes may cover performance requirements for carriage services using optical fibre

sections.source_quote: Examples of matters that may be dealt with by industry codes and industry standards: (a) telling customers about: (i) goods or services on offer; (ii) the prices of those goods or services; (iii) the other terms and conditions on which those goods or services are offered; (b) giving customers information about performance indicators customers can use to evaluate the quality of services; (c) regular reporting to customers about performance against those performance indicators; (d) the internal handling of customer complaints; (e) reporting about customer complaints; (f) privacy and, in particular: (i) the protection of personal information; and

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Procedures for unsolicited commercial electronic messages
sections.summary: This section specifies requirements for handling unsolicited commercial electronic messages, including server configuration to prevent sending, shutdown of open relay servers, responsible practices for minors, consent procedures for electronic account holders, telemarketing call record-keeping, and limits on telemarketing calls and faxes.

requirements.text: Procedures to minimize or prevent sending or delivery of unsolicited commercial electronic messages
Shutdown of open relay servers
Action to ensure responsible practices for marketing to individuals under 18
Procedures for obtaining consent from electronic account holders
Record-keeping practices for telemarketing calls made or attempted
Action to limit telemarketing calls attempted per period per number

sections.source_quote: action to be taken to minimize or prevent the sending or delivery of unsolicited commercial electronic messages, including: (i) the configuration of servers so as to minimise or prevent the sending or delivery of unsolicited commercial electronic messages; and (ii) the shutdown of open relay servers;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Limits on telemarketing calls and faxes
sections.summary: This section sets limits on the number of telemarketing calls and faxes that can be made or attempted per period to a specific Australian number, with the goal of reducing call volume and ensuring compliance with industry standards.

requirements.text: Action to limit telemarketing calls made or attempted per period to a specific Australian number
Record-keeping practices for marketing faxes sent or attempted
Action to limit marketing faxes sent or attempted per period to a specific Australian number

sections.source_quote: action to be taken to limit the total number of telemarketing calls made, or attempted to be made, by a particular participant in a section of the telemarketing industry, during a particular period to a particular Australian number;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: ACMA maintains Register of industry codes and standards
sections.summary: The ACMA must maintain a Register containing all industry codes, industry standards, requests under section 118, notices under section 119, and directions under section 121. The Register may be accessed electronically with payment of a fee, and the ACMA may provide electronic copies of the Register.

requirements.text: ACMA must maintain a Register of industry codes and standards
ACMA must include all industry codes in force in the Register
ACMA must include all industry standards in the Register
ACMA must include all requests made under section 118 in the Register
ACMA must include all notices under section 119 in the Register
ACMA must include all directions given under section 121 in the Register
The Register may be maintained electronically
A person may inspect the Register after paying a fee
A person may make a copy of the Register after paying a fee
ACMA may provide electronic copies of the Register

sections.source_quote: The ACMA is to maintain a Register in which the ACMA includes: (a) all industry codes required to be registered under this Part, as those codes are in force from time to time; and (b) all industry standards; and (c) all requests made under section 118; and (d) all notices under section 119; and (e) all directions given under section 121.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: ACMA may request additional information for industry code applications
sections.summary: The ACMA may request additional information from applicants within 20 business days of an application, and may refuse to consider the application until the applicant provides the requested information.

requirements.text: ACMA may request additional information from applicants within 20 business days
ACMA may refuse to consider the application until additional information is provided

sections.source_quote: The ACMA may, within 20 business days after an application is made, request the applicant to give the ACMA, within the period specified in the request, further information about the application.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: ACMA approval conditions for industry code variations
sections.summary: The ACMA must approve industry code variations if the body or association represents the relevant telecommunications industry section, the code is registered, it addresses retail customer relationships, the variation process ensures customer interests, and the refundable costs are reasonable. The ACMA can refuse approval if any condition is not met. Approval is irrevocable and lasts two years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets conditions for ACMA approval of industry code variations, requiring customer interest representation and cost reasonableness. Relevant for DTIAs involving telecommunications industry code updates.

requirements.text: The body or association represents the section of the telecommunications industry
The code is registered under this Part
The code deals wholly or mainly with retail customer relationships
The variation process ensures retail customer interests are adequately represented
The total refundable costs for varying the code are reasonable

sections.source_quote: If the ACMA is not satisfied as to the matters set out in subsection (2A), the ACMA must, by written notice given to the applicant, refuse to make the declaration.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reimbursement of costs for industry code development
sections.summary: The ACMA determines reimbursement for costs incurred by bodies or associations developing industry codes if specific conditions are met, including code relevance to retail customers, adequate representation of customer interests, and proper documentation of costs with auditor verification. Reimbursement is limited to the lesser of actual costs or the estimate from the initial application.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs reimbursement for industry code development costs, requiring customer interest representation and auditor verification. Relevant for DTIAs involving telecommunications industry code development.

requirements.text: A section 136B declaration was made for code development
The body provided the code to ACMA under section 117
The code deals with retail customer relationships
The development process ensured customer interests
The code copy included cost statement, reimbursement claim, auditor declaration, and process description
Each cost is a refundable cost incurred during the declaration period

sections.source_quote: If: (a) a section 136B declaration was made... (b) when the section 136B declaration was in force, the body or association gave a copy of the code to the ACMA...

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reimbursement of costs for industry code variations
sections.summary: The ACMA determines reimbursement for costs incurred by bodies or associations varying industry codes if specific conditions are met, including code relevance to retail customers, adequate representation of customer interests, and proper documentation of costs with auditor verification. Reimbursement is limited to the lesser of actual costs or the estimate from the initial application and must be paid within 30 days.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs reimbursement for industry code variation costs, requiring customer interest representation and auditor verification. Relevant for DTIAs involving telecommunications industry code updates.

requirements.text: A section 136B declaration was made for code variation
The body provided the variation to ACMA under section 119A
The code deals with retail customer relationships
The variation process ensured customer interests
The variation copy included cost statement, reimbursement claim, auditor declaration, and process description
Each cost is a refundable cost incurred during the declaration period

sections.source_quote: If: (a) a section 136B declaration was made in relation to the variation of an industry code by a body or association; and (b) when the section 136B declaration was in force, the body or association gave a copy of the variation to the ACMA under section 119A;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reimbursement of costs for industry code variations
sections.summary: The ACMA determines reimbursement for costs incurred by bodies or associations varying industry codes if specific conditions are met, including code relevance to retail customers, adequate representation of customer interests, and proper documentation of costs with auditor verification. Reimbursement is limited to the lesser of actual costs or the estimate from the initial application and must be paid within 30 days.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs reimbursement for industry code variation costs, requiring customer interest representation and auditor verification. Relevant for DTIAs involving telecommunications industry code updates.

requirements.text: The ACMA determines entitlement to a specified reimbursement amount
The specified amount is the lesser of actual costs or the estimate from the initial application
The ACMA pays the amount within 30 days of notification

sections.source_quote: The ACMA must, by written notice given to the body or association, determine that the body or association is entitled to be paid a specified amount.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definitions for telecommunications industry terms
sections.summary: This section defines specific terms used in telecommunications regulation, including 'retailer' in joint functional separation contexts, 'sewerage services body', 'state or territory road authority', 'storm water drainage services', 'superfast carriage service', 'supply', 'unsatisfactory compliance record', and 'upgrade' for telecommunications networks. These definitions are critical for understanding industry-specific regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish industry-specific terminology for telecommunications regulation, relevant for DTIAs involving service providers and network infrastructure.

requirements.text: Retailer means a person identified in the undertaking as the retailer
Sewerage services body has the same meaning as in the National Broadband Network Companies Act 2011
State or territory road authority has the same meaning as in the National Broadband Network Companies Act 2011
Storm water drainage services has the same meaning as in the National Broadband Network Companies Act 2011
Storm water drainage services body has the same meaning as in the National Broadband Network Companies Act 2011
Superfast carriage service requires download speed of 25 megabits per second or more
Supply includes supply by a corporation to itself
Unsatisfactory compliance record has meaning affected by section 142BC
Upgrade has meaning affected by section 160

sections.source_quote: retailer, when used in relation to a joint functional separation undertaking, means a person identified in the undertaking as the retailer, or one of the retailers, for the purposes of the undertaking.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Terms and conditions for functional separation
sections.summary: This section defines terms and conditions for functional separation undertakings, treating notional contracts between business units as actual contracts and their terms as actual terms. It also specifies that unsatisfactory compliance records are determined based on breaches of functional separation undertakings and contraventions of specific sections related to functional separation.
sections.dtia_summary: These provisions define how functional separation undertakings are treated in regulatory contexts, relevant for DTIAs involving telecommunications service providers.

requirements.text: Notional contracts between business units are treated as actual contracts
Terms and conditions in notional contracts are treated as actual terms
Unsatisfactory compliance records are determined based on breaches of functional separation undertakings
Unsatisfactory compliance records consider contraventions of specific sections

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Part: (a) a notional contract (however described) between a corporation's business units is to be treated as if it were an actual contract; and (b) any terms and conditions (whether or not relating to price or a method of ascertaining price) in such a notional contract are to be treated as if they were actual terms and conditions.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Eligible service activities and non-discrimination
sections.summary: This section defines activities that may be considered for eligibility under the Act, including planning for facilities, preparatory activities, and ancillary services. It prohibits discrimination in favor of the entity itself when engaging in eligible service development, enhancement, or network capability improvements. The section specifies that such discrimination must not occur in relation to developing new services, enhancing existing services, or extending network capabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines activities that may be considered for eligibility under the Act and prohibits discrimination in favor of the entity itself when engaging in eligible service development, enhancement, or network capability improvements. It does not directly impact data transfers but may affect service provider relationships.

requirements.text: Planning for a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied
An activity that is preparatory to the supply of an eligible service
An activity that is ancillary or incidental to the supply of an eligible service
Giving information to service providers about any of the above activities
The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to developing a new eligible service
The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to enhancing an eligible service

The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to extending or enhancing the capability of a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied

sections.source_quote: 192 Compilation (iv) planning for a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied; (v) an activity that is preparatory to the supply of an eligible service; (vi) an activity that is ancillary or incidental to the supply of an eligible service; (vii) giving information to service providers about any of the above activities; (e) the person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to the carrying on of any of the following activities: (i) developing a new eligible service; (ii) enhancing an eligible service; (iii) extending or enhancing the capability of a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied; (iv) planning for a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied; (v) an activity that is preparatory to the supply of an eligible service; (vi) an activity that is ancillary or incidental to the supply of an eligible service; (vii) giving information to service providers about any of the above activities;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Wholesale customer discrimination grounds
sections.summary: This section outlines grounds for believing that a wholesale customer or prospective customer would fail to comply with terms and conditions for designated carriage services. It specifies that such grounds include evidence of poor creditworthiness or repeated failures to comply with service terms. The section is part of a regulatory framework for wholesale customers in the telecommunications sector.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines grounds for believing that a wholesale customer or prospective customer would fail to comply with terms and conditions for designated carriage services. It does not directly impact data transfers but may affect service provider relationships.

requirements.text: Evidence that the wholesale customer or prospective wholesale customer is not creditworthy
Repeated failures by the wholesale customer or prospective wholesale customer to comply with the terms and conditions on which the person supplied eligible services

sections.source_quote: 193 Compilation material extent, to comply with the terms and conditions on which the person supplies designated carriage services. (5) Examples of grounds for believing as mentioned in subsection (4) include: (a) evidence that the wholesale customer or prospective wholesale customer is not creditworthy; and (b) repeated failures by the wholesale customer or prospective wholesale customer to comply with the terms and conditions on which the person supplied eligible services (whether or not using the line).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Joint functional separation undertakings and cancellations
sections.summary: This section details the conditions under which a joint functional separation undertaking can be canceled. It specifies that the corporation may give the ACCC a joint functional separation undertaking, and that the undertaking is canceled immediately before the undertaking comes into force. The section also outlines the process for the ACCC to make determinations under this section.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the conditions under which a joint functional separation undertaking can be canceled. It does not directly impact data transfers but may affect service provider relationships.

requirements.text: The corporation may give the ACCC a joint functional separation undertaking
The corporation is taken to have cancelled the election immediately before the joint functional separation undertaking comes into force

sections.source_quote: 214 Compilation (a) this Act does not prevent the corporation, together with one or more other persons, from giving the ACCC a joint functional separation undertaking; (b) if the joint functional separation undertaking comes into force—the corporation is taken to have cancelled the election immediately before the joint functional separation undertaking comes into force.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Wholesaler and retailer separation requirements
sections.summary: This section outlines specific requirements for separating wholesalers and retailers, including ensuring no directors are shared between entities, having separate operational and business systems, and publishing terms on the website. It mandates that wholesalers provide local access line services to customers on published terms and ensure customer information is not disclosed to retailers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines specific requirements for separating wholesalers and retailers, including ensuring no directors are shared between entities, having separate operational and business systems, and publishing terms on the website. It does not directly impact data transfers but may affect service provider relationships.

requirements.text: A wholesaler will ensure that no director of the wholesaler is a director of a retailer
A retailer will ensure that no director of the retailer is a director of a wholesaler
A wholesaler will have separate operational support systems
A wholesaler will have separate business systems
A wholesaler will have separate communications systems
A wholesaler will have separate accounts
A wholesaler will publish on the wholesaler's website terms for supplying local access line services to retailers and prospective wholesale customers
A wholesaler will supply local access line services to wholesale customers on the terms and conditions published on the website
A wholesaler will ensure that information provided by wholesale customers (other than retailers) is not disclosed to retailers

sections.source_quote: 216 Compilation (h) provide that a wholesaler will ensure that no director of the wholesaler is a director of a retailer; and (i) provide that a retailer will ensure that no director of the retailer is a director of a wholesaler; and (j) provide that: (i) the wholesaler or wholesalers; and (ii) the retailer or retailers; will, to the extent specified in the undertaking, have separate: (iii) operational support systems; (iv) business systems; (v) communications systems; (vi) accounts; and (k) provide that a wholesaler will publish on the wholesaler's website: on which the wholesaler offers to supply local access line services to the following: (iii) a retailer; (iv) its wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers; and (l) provide that a wholesaler will: (i) if requested to do so by a wholesale customer or prospective wholesale customer, supply a local access line service to the wholesale customer or prospective wholesale customer; and (ii) do so on the terms and conditions that were published on the wholesaler's website at the time when the request was made; and (m) provide that a wholesaler will ensure that information provided by its wholesale customers (other than the retailer or retailers) is not disclosed to any of the retailers;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Revocation of functional separation undertakings
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which the ACCC may revoke a joint functional separation undertaking, including breaches of fundamental provisions, contraventions of specific sections, or unsatisfactory compliance records. It specifies the time limits for revocation and the effect of revocation on local access line services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines the conditions under which the ACCC may revoke a joint functional separation undertaking, including breaches of fundamental provisions, contraventions of specific sections, or unsatisfactory compliance records. It does not directly impact data transfers but may affect service provider relationships.

requirements.text: The ACCC may revoke the undertaking if any of those persons has breached a fundamental provision of the undertaking
The ACCC may revoke the undertaking if any of those persons has contravened section 151ZF or 1.51ZG
The ACCC may revoke the undertaking if any of those persons has an unsatisfactory compliance record in relation to functional separation
The revocation takes effect at the time specified in the notice of revocation, which must not be earlier than the time the notice is given
The revocation takes effect at the time specified in the notice of revocation, which must not be later than 12 months after the notice is given
The undertaking does not apply to local access line services supplied using a local access line that came into existence after the notice was given

sections.source_quote: 238 Compilation (b) any of those persons has: (i) breached a fundamental provision of the undertaking; or (ii) contravened section 151ZF or 151ZG; the ACCC may, by written notice given to each of those persons, revoke the undertaking.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Consultation on revocation of functional separation undertakings
sections.summary: This section details the consultation process required before the ACCC can revoke a standard functional separation undertaking. It mandates that the ACCC provide written notice to the person, inviting submissions within a 15 business day timeframe, and consider any submissions received during this period. The section also outlines the conditions for revocation of joint functional separation undertakings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the consultation process required before the ACCC can revoke a standard functional separation undertaking. It does not directly impact data transfers but may affect service provider relationships.

requirements.text: The ACCC must give the person a written notice stating that the ACCC proposes to revoke the standard functional separation undertaking
The ACCC must invite the person to make a submission to the ACCC about the revocation within the time limit specified in the notice
The time limit must not be shorter than 15 business days after the notice is given

sections.source_quote: 239 Compilation (1) Before making a decision under subsection 151W(1) or (2) to revoke a standard functional separation undertaking given by a person, the ACCC must: (a) give the person a written notice: (i) stating that the ACCC proposes to revoke the [undertaking]; (ii) inviting the person to make a submission to the ACCC about the revocation within the time limit specified in the notice; and (b) consider any submission received within the time limit

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Notification of proposed revocation of functional separation undertaking
sections.summary: This section requires the ACCC to provide written notice to persons who have given functional separation undertakings when proposing to revoke such undertakings. It also mandates that those persons submit written submissions to the ACCC within 15 business days of receiving the notice. The notice must specify the revocation proposal and the submission deadline. This applies to breaches of functional separation undertakings that occurred while the undertaking was in force and contraventions of specific sections.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a notification process for revocation of functional separation undertakings, requiring timely submissions to the ACCC. It may impact DTIAs by creating procedural requirements for notifying authorities about changes in compliance status.

requirements.text: ACCC must give written notice to persons who gave functional separation undertakings proposing revocation
Persons must submit written submissions to ACCC about revocation within 15 business days

sections.source_quote: 240 (a) give each of those persons a written notice: (i) stating that the ACCC proposes to revoke the (ii) inviting those persons to make submissions to the ACCC about the revocation within the time limit

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Compliance with functional separation direction
sections.summary: This section mandates that persons must comply with directions issued by the ACCC under functional separation undertakings. It specifies that non-compliance with these directions constitutes a contravention subject to civil penalties. The section applies to breaches of functional separation undertakings and contraventions of specific sections related to functional separation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires compliance with ACCC directions regarding functional separation, which may affect DTIAs through enforcement mechanisms for non-compliance with cross-border data handling obligations.

requirements.text: A person must comply with a direction under subsection (1) or (2)

sections.source_quote: 244 (3) A person must comply with a direction under subsection (1) or (2).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Notification of changes in control for functional separation undertakings
sections.summary: This section requires persons who have given functional separation undertakings to notify the ACCC in writing within 10 business days if they become aware of changes in control. It covers both the person who gave the undertaking and others who become aware of control changes. The notices must be in a form approved by the ACCC and must be submitted as soon as practicable.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates timely notification of control changes for functional separation undertakings, which may impact DTIAs by requiring specific reporting mechanisms for changes in data processing authority.

requirements.text: First person must notify ACCC in writing within 10 business days of becoming aware of control changes
Other person must notify ACCC in writing within 10 business days of becoming aware of control changes

sections.source_quote: 245 (c) notify the ACCC, in writing, of that event; and (d) do so as soon as practicable, but not later than 10 business days, after becoming so aware.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Enforcement of functional separation undertakings by Federal Court
sections.summary: This section allows the Federal Court to issue orders to enforce functional separation undertakings when breaches are confirmed. The court may direct compliance with the undertaking, dispose of assets, restrain share rights, prohibit payments, disregard share rights, require compensation for financial benefits obtained from breaches, or make other appropriate orders. These orders apply to persons who have given functional separation undertakings in force.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides enforcement mechanisms for functional separation undertakings, which may impact DTIAs by creating potential legal remedies for breaches of cross-border data handling obligations.

requirements.text: Federal Court may make orders directing compliance with functional separation undertaking
Federal Court may order disposal of network units, lines, shares or other assets
Federal Court may order restraint on exercise of share rights
Federal Court may prohibit or defer payment of sums due to a person for shares
Federal Court may disregard exercise of rights attached to shares
Federal Court may order payment to Commonwealth for financial benefits obtained from breach

sections.source_quote: 247 (1) If: (a) a functional separation undertaking given by a person is in force; and (b) the Federal Court is satisfied, on the application of: (i) the ACCC; or (ii) a carrier; or (iii) a carriage service provider; that the person has breached the undertaking; the Court may make any or all of the following orders: (c) an order directing the person to comply with the undertaking; (d) an order directing the disposal of network units, lines, shares or other assets; (e) an order restraining the exercise of any rights attached to shares; (f) an order prohibiting or deferring the payment of any sums due to a person in respect of shares held by the person; (g) an order that any exercise of rights attached to shares be disregarded; (h) an order directing the person to pay to the Commonwealth an amount up to the amount of any financial benefit that the person has obtained directly or indirectly and that is reasonably attributable to the breach;

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Non-discrimination in supply of eligible services
sections.summary: This section prohibits discrimination between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers when supplying eligible services using a local access line. It allows exceptions for customers with reasonable grounds to believe they would fail to comply with terms and conditions, such as credit issues or repeated non-compliance. The rule applies to services supplied or proposed to be supplied after the designated commencement date.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures non-discrimination in service provision, which may impact DTIAs by requiring equal treatment of data subjects across different customer segments.

requirements.text: Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in supply of eligible services
Exception for customers with reasonable grounds to believe they would fail to comply with terms and conditions

sections.source_quote: 249 (1) If: (a) an eligible service is supplied, or proposed to be supplied, by a person using a local access line; and [the person must not, on or after the designated commencement date, discriminate between the person's wholesale customers, or the person's prospective wholesale customers, in relation to the supply of eligible services using the line.]

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Non-discrimination in related activities for eligible services
sections.summary: This section prohibits discrimination between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in related activities such as developing new services, enhancing existing services, extending network capabilities, planning facilities, and preparatory or ancillary activities. The rule applies to persons supplying eligible services using a local access line after the designated commencement date.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures non-discrimination in related activities, which may impact DTIAs by requiring equal treatment of data subjects across service development and network activities.

requirements.text: Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in developing new eligible services
Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in enhancing eligible services
Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in extending or enhancing network capabilities
Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in planning facilities
Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in preparatory activities
Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in ancillary activities
Person must not discriminate between wholesale customers or prospective wholesale customers in giving information about activities

sections.source_quote: 251 (2) The person must not, in carrying on any of the following activities, discriminate between the person's wholesale customers or the person's prospective wholesale customers: (a) developing a new eligible service; (b) enhancing an eligible service; (c) extending or enhancing the capability of a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied; (d) planning for a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied; (e) an activity that is preparatory to the supply of an eligible service; (f) an activity that is ancillary or incidental to the supply of an eligible service; (g) giving information to service providers about any of the above activities.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Prohibition on self-discrimination in service development and network enhancement
sections.summary: A person must not discriminate in favor of itself when developing, enhancing, extending, or planning eligible services, facilities, or telecommunications networks. This prohibition also covers preparatory and ancillary activities related to service supply. The rule applies to entities engaged in eligible service provision and requires non-discriminatory treatment across all activities listed.

requirements.text: The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to developing a new eligible service
The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to enhancing an eligible service

The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to extending or enhancing the capability of a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied
The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to planning for a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied
The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to an activity that is preparatory to the supply of an eligible service
The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to an activity that is ancillary or incidental to the supply of an eligible service
The person must not discriminate in favour of itself in relation to giving information to service providers about any of the above activities

sections.source_quote: No discrimination by a person in favour of itself in relation to the carrying on of any of the following activities: (a) developing a new eligible service; (b) enhancing an eligible service; (c) extending or enhancing the capability of a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied; (d) planning for a facility or telecommunications network by means of which an eligible service is, or is to be, supplied; (e) an activity that is preparatory to the supply of an eligible service; (f) an activity that is ancillary or incidental to the supply of an eligible service; (g) giving information to service providers about any of the above activities.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Protection of communications information for carriers and service providers
sections.summary: Carriers, carriage service providers, number-database operators, emergency call persons, and their associates must protect the confidentiality of communications content, carriage services, and other persons' personal particulars. Disclosure is permitted only for limited purposes such as criminal law enforcement, and authorized recipients may only use information for authorized purposes with specific record-keeping requirements.

requirements.text: Carriers, carriage service providers, number-database operators, emergency call persons and their respective associates must protect the confidentiality of information that relates to the contents of communications that have been, or are being, carried by carriers or carriage service providers

Carriers, carriage service providers, number-database operators, emergency call persons and their respective associates must protect the confidentiality of information that relates to carriage services supplied by carriers and carriage service providers

Carriers, carriage service providers, number-database operators, emergency call persons and their respective associates must protect the confidentiality of information that relates to the affairs or personal particulars of other persons
The disclosure or use of protected information is authorised in limited circumstances (for example, disclosure or use for purposes relating to the enforcement of the criminal law)
An authorised recipient of protected information may only disclose or use the information for an authorised purpose
Certain record-keeping requirements are imposed in relation to authorised disclosures or uses of information

sections.source_quote: Carriers, carriage service providers, number-database operators, emergency call persons and their respective associates must protect the confidentiality of information that relates to: (a) the contents of communications that have been, or are being, carried by carriers or carriage service providers; and (b) carriage services supplied by carriers and carriage service providers; and (c) the affairs or personal particulars of other persons.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Definition of location information for mobile devices
sections.summary: Information about the location of a mobile telephone handset or any other mobile communications device is treated as relating to the customer responsible for the device. Documents containing such location information are also considered to relate to the customer's affairs. This definition applies to the context of telecommunications services.

requirements.text: Information about the location of a mobile telephone handset is taken to be information that relates to the affairs of the customer responsible for the handset
Information about the location of any other mobile communications device is taken to be information that relates to the affairs of the customer responsible for the device

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Part, information about the location of: (a) a mobile telephone handset; or (b) any other mobile communications device; is taken to be information that relates to the affairs of the customer responsible for the handset or device.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Scope of electromagnetic energy carriage for communications
sections.summary: Subparagraphs (1)(a)(i) and (ii) do not apply to communications carried by carriers or carriage service providers unless the carriage was by means of guided and/or unguided electromagnetic energy. This section defines the scope of electromagnetic energy carriage in telecommunications.

requirements.text: Subparagraphs (1)(a)(i) and (ii) do not apply to a communication that is or has been carried by a carrier or carriage service provider unless the carriage was by means of, is by means of, or is proposed to be delivered by means of, guided and/or unguided electromagnetic energy

sections.source_quote: Subparagraphs (1)(a)(i) and (ii) do not apply to a communication that is or has been carried by a carrier or carriage service provider unless the carriage was by means of, is by means of, or is proposed to be delivered by means of, guided and/or unguided electromagnetic energy.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exceptions to primary disclosure/use offences for employees
sections.summary: Disclosures or uses of protected information by employees of carriers, carriage service providers, telecommunications contractors, or number-database operators are exempt from the primary disclosure/use offences when performed in the course of their duties. This exception applies to both employees and contractors in specific telecommunications contexts.

requirements.text: Section 276 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of the person is an employee of a carrier in the performance of the person's duties as such an employee
Section 276 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of the person is an employee of a carriage service provider in the performance of the person's duties as such an employee
Section 276 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of the person is an employee of a telecommunications contractor in the performance of the person's duties as such an employee
Section 276 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of the person is a telecommunications contractor in the performance of the person's duties as such a contractor
Section 277 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of the person is an employee of a number-database operator in the performance of the person's duties as such an employee
Section 277 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of the person is an employee of a number-database contractor in the performance of the person's duties as such an employee
Section 277 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of the person is a number-database contractor in the performance of the person's duties as such a contractor

sections.source_quote: Section 276 does not prohibit a disclosure or use by a person of (a) the person is an employee of: (i) a carrier; or (ii) a carriage service provider; or (iii) a telecommunications contractor; and (b) the disclosure or use is made in the performance of the person's duties as such an employee.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Exceptions for compliance with telecommunications interception laws
sections.summary: Disclosures of information to service providers for compliance with the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 are permitted for specific purposes such as law enforcement, warrant compliance, or customer access to personal information. These disclosures must not be used for any other purpose beyond the authorized scope.

requirements.text: The information or document is kept, by a service provider, solely for the purpose of complying with Part 5-1A of the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979

The information or document is not used or disclosed by the service provider for any purpose other than one or more of the following purposes: (i) complying with Part 5-1A of that Act; (ii) complying with the requirements of warrants under Chapters 2 and 3 of that Act or authorisations under Chapter 4 of that Act; (iii) complying with requests or requirements to make disclosures provided for by sections 284 to 288 of this Act; (iv) providing persons with access to their personal information in accordance with the Privacy Act 1988; (v) a purpose prescribed by the regulations; (vi) a purpose incidental to any of the purposes referred to in subparagraphs (i) to (v)

sections.source_quote: The information or document is kept, by a service provider (within the meaning of the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979), solely for the purpose of complying with Part 5-1A of that Act; and the information or document is not used or disclosed by the service provider for any purpose other than one or more of the following purposes: (i) complying with Part  5-1A of that Act; (ii) complying with the requirements of warrants under Chapters 2 and 3 of that Act or authorisations under Chapter 4 of that Act; (iii) complying with requests or requirements to make disclosures provided for by sections 284 to 288 of this Act; (iv) providing persons with access to their personal information in accordance with the Privacy Act 1988; (v) a purpose prescribed by the regulations; (vi) a purpose incidental to any of the purposes referred to in subparagraphs (i) to (v).

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Witness disclosures exempt from privacy prohibitions
sections.summary: This section exempts disclosures made by witnesses in civil proceedings from the Privacy Act's prohibitions if the information is held solely for complying with the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 and used only for specific purposes like law enforcement, warrant compliance, or other authorized disclosures under the Act. The exemption applies to disclosures made by witnesses in civil proceedings but excludes disclosures to enforcement agencies or during implementation phases.
sections.dtia_summary: Exempts witness disclosures from privacy prohibitions when information is used for lawful telecommunications purposes under the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving law enforcement.

requirements.text: Disclosures by witnesses in civil proceedings are exempt from privacy prohibitions if information is held solely for complying with Part 5-1A of the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979
Information must not be used for any purpose other than complying with the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979, warrant compliance, or authorized disclosures under the Act
Exemption does not apply to disclosures to enforcement agencies or during implementation phases

sections.source_quote: 281 (1) Division 2 does not prohibit a disclosure by a person of information or a document if the person makes the disclosure as a witness summoned to give evidence or to produce documents.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Permitted disclosures for directory services and emergency calls
sections.summary: This section permits disclosures of information to other persons for directory assistance services, public number directories, or research under specific instruments, provided the recipient holds authorization under the integrated public number database scheme. It also exempts emergency call handling from privacy prohibitions when information relates to emergency service numbers and it is unreasonable to obtain consent.
